title: Differentiate Everything with a Reversible Embeded Domain-Specific Language [PDF]

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## Differentiate Everything with a Reversible Embeded Domain-Specific Language

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## Abstract

Reverse-mode automatic differentiation (AD) suffers from the issue of having too much space overhead to trace back intermediate computational states for back-propagation. The traditional method to trace back states is called checkpointing that stores intermediate states into a global stack and restore state through either stack pop or re-computing. The overhead of stack manipulations and re-computing makes the general purposed (not tensor-based) AD engines unable to meet many industrial needs. Instead of checkpointing, we propose to use reverse computing to trace back states by designing and implementing a reversible programming eDSL, where a program can be executed bi-directionally without implicit stack operations. The absence of implicit stack operations makes the program compatible with existing compiler features, including utilizing existing optimization passes and compiling the code as GPU kernels. We implement AD for sparse matrix operations and some machine learning applications to show that our framework has the state-of-the-art performance.

## 1 Introduction

Most of the popular automatic differentiation (AD) tools in the market, such as TensorFlow (Abadi et al., 2015), Pytorch (Paszke et al., 2017), and Flux (Innes et al., 2018) implements reverse mode AD at the tensor level to meet the need in machine learning. Later, People in the scientific computing domain also realized the power of these AD tools, they use these tools to solve scientific problems such as seismic inversion (Zhu et al., 2020), variational quantum circuits simulation (Bergholm et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2019) and variational tensor network simulation (Liao et al., 2019; Roberts et al., 2019). To meet the diverse need in these applications, one sometimes has to define backward rules manually, for example

1. To differentiate sparse matrix operations used in Hamiltonian engineering (Hao Xie & Wang), people defined backward rules for sparse matrix multiplication and dominant eigensolvers (Golub & Van Loan, 2012),

2. In tensor network algorithms to study the phase transition problem (Liao et al., 2019; Seeger et al., 2017; Wan & Zhang, 2019; Hubig, 2019), people defined backward rules for singular value decomposition (SVD) function and QR decomposition (Golub & Van Loan, 2012).

Instead of defining backward rules manually, one can also use a general purposed AD (GP-AD) framework like Tapenade (Hascoet & Pascual, 2013), OpenAD (Utke et al., 2008) and Zygote (Innes, 2018; Innes et al., 2019). Researchers have used these tools in practical applications such as bundle adjustment (Shen & Dai, 2018) and earth system simulation (Forget et al., 2015), where differentiating scalar operations is important. However, the power of these tools are often limited by their relatively poor performance. In many practical applications, a program might do billions of computations. In each computational step, the AD engine might cache some data for backpropagation. (Griewank & Walther, 2008) Frequent caching of data slows down the program significantly, while the memory usage will become a bottleneck as well. Caching implicitly also make these frameworks incompatible with kernel functions. To avoid such issues, we need a new GP-AD framework that does not cache automatically for users.
In this paper, we propose to implement the reverse mode AD on a reversible (domain-specific) programming language (Perumalla, 2013; Frank, 2017), where intermediate states can be traced

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

backward without accessing an implicit stack. Reversible programming allows people to utilize the reversibility to reverse a program. In machine learning, reversibility is proven to substantially decrease the memory usage in unitary recurrent neural networks (MacKay et al., 2018), normalizing flow (Dinh et al., 2014), hyper-parameter learning (Maclaurin et al., 2015) and residual neural networks (Gomez et al., 2017; Behrmann et al., 2018). Reversible programming will make these happen naturally. The power of reversible programming is not limited to handling these reversible applications, any program can be written in a reversible style. Converting an irreversible program to the reversible form would cost overheads in time and space. Reversible programming provides a flexible time-space trade-off scheme that different with checkpointing (Griewank, 1992; Griewank & Walther, 2008; Chen et al., 2016), _reverse computing_ (Bennett, 1989; Levine & Sherman, 1990), to let user handle these overheads explicitly. There have been many prototypes of reversible languages like Janus (Lutz, 1986), R (not the popular one) (Frank, 1997), Erlang (Lanese et al., 2018) and object-oriented ROOPL (Haulund, 2017). In the past, the primary motivation to study reversible programming is to support reversible computing devices (Frank & Knight Jr, 1999) such as adiabatic complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor (CMOS) (Koller & Athas, 1992), molecular mechanical computing system (Merkle et al., 2018) and superconducting system (Likharev, 1977; Semenov et al., 2003; Takeuchi et al., 2014; 2017), and these reversible computing devices are orders more energy-efficient. Landauer proves that only when a device does not erase information (i.e. reversible), its energy efficiency can go beyond the thermal dynamic limit. (Landauer, 1961; Reeb & Wolf, 2014) However, these reversible programming languages can not be used directly in real scientific computing, since most of them do not have basic elements like floating point numbers, arrays, and complex numbers. This motivates us to build a new embedded domain-specific language (eDSL) in Julia (Bezanson et al., 2012; 2017) as a new playground of GP-AD.

In this paper, we first compare the time-space trade-off in the optimal checkpointing and the optimal reverse computing in Sec. 2. Then we introduce the language design of NiLang in Sec. 3. In Sec. 4, we explain the implementation of automatic differentiation in NiLang. In Sec. 5, we benchmark the performance of NiLang’s AD with other AD software and explain why it is fast.

## 2 Reverse computing as an Alternative to Checkpointing

One can use either checkpointing or reverse computing to trace back intermediate states of a _T_ - step computational process _s_ 1 = _f_ 1( _s_ 0), _s_ 2 = _f_ 2( _s_ 1), . . . , _sT_ = _fT_ ( _sT_ −1) with a run-time memory _S_ . In the checkpointing scheme, the program first takes snapshots of states at certain time steps _S_ = { _sa_ , _sb_ , . . .}, 1 ≤ _a_ < _b_ < ... ≤ _T_ by running a forward pass. When retrieving a state _sk_ , if _sk_ ∈ _S_ , just return this state, otherwise, return max _s j_ < _k_ ∈ _S_ and re-compute _sk_ from _s j_ . In the reverse _j_ computing scheme, one first writes the program in a reversible style. Without prior knowledge, a regular program can be transpiled to the reversible style is by doing the transformation in Listing. 1.

Listing 1: Transpiling a regular code to the reversible code without prior knowledge.

Listing 2: The reverse of Listing. 1

Then one can visit states in the reversed order by running the reversed program in Listing. 2, which erases the computed results from the tail. One may argue that easing through uncomputing is not necessary here. This is not true for a general reversible program, because the intermediate states might be mutable and used in other parts of the program. It is easy to see, both checkpointing and reverse computing can trace back states without time overhead, but both suffer from a space overhead that linear to time (Table 1). The checkpointing scheme snapshots the output in every step, and the reverse computing scheme allocates extra storage for storing outputs in every step. On the other side, only checkpointing can achieve a zero space overhead by recomputing everything from the beginning _s_ 0, with a time complexity _O_ ( _T_[2] ). The difference in space overheads can be explained by the difference of the optimal checkpointing and optimal reverse computing algorithms. The optimal checkpointing algorithm that widely used in AD is the treeverse algorithm in Fig. 1(a). This algorithm partitions the computational process **binomially** into _d_ sectors. At the beginning of each sector, the program snapshots the state and push it into a global stack, hence the memory for checkpointing is _dS_ . The states in the last sector are retrieved by the above space-efficient _O_ ( _T_[2] ) algorithm. After that, the last snapshot can be freed and the program has one more quota in memory. With the freed memory, the second last sector can be further partition into two sectors. Likewise, the _l_ th sectors is partitioned into _l_ sub-sectors, where _l_ is the sector index counting from the tail. Recursively apply this treeverse algorithm _t_ times until the sector size is 1. The approximated overhead in time and space are
where _T_ = η( _t_ , _d_ ) holds. By carefully choosing either a _t_ or _d_ , the overhead in time and space can be both logarithmic.

On the other side, the optimal time-space trade-off scheme in reverse computing is the Bennett’s algorithm illustrated in Fig. 1 (b). It evenly **evenly** partition the program into _k_ sectors. The program marches forward ( _P_ process) for _k_ steps to obtain the final state _sk_ +1, then backward ( _Q_ process) from the _k_ − 1th step to erase the states in between _s_ 1< _i_ < _k_ . This process is also called the _compute-copyuncompute_ process. Recursively apply the compute-copy-uncompute process for each sector until each _P_ / _Q_ process contains only one unit computation. the time and space complexities are

Here, the overhead in time is polynomial, which is worse than the treeverse algorithm. The treeverse like partition does not apply here because the first sweep to create initial checkpoints without introducing any space overheads is not possible in reversible computing. In Appendix B.2, we show how to implement a unitary matrix multiplication without introducing overheads in space and time. _Second_ , reverse computing does not allocate automatically for users, user can optimize the memory access patterns for their own devices like GPU. _Third_ , reverse computing is compatible with effective codes, so that it fits better with modern languages. In Appendix B.3, we show reversible thinking can lead the user to a constant memory, constant time implementation of chained multiplication algorithms.

## 3 Language design

NiLang is an embedded domain-specific language (eDSL) NiLang built on top of the host language Julia (Bezanson et al., 2012; 2017). Julia is a popular language for scientific programming and machine learning. We choose Julia mainly for speed. Julia is a language with high abstraction, however, its clever design of type inference and just in time compiling make it has a C like speed. Meanwhile, it has rich features for meta-programming. Its package for pattern matching MLStyle allows us to define an eDSL in less than 2000 lines. Comparing with a regular reversible programming language, NiLang features array operations, rich number systems including floating-point numbers, complex numbers, fixed-point numbers, and logarithmic numbers. It also implements the compute-copy-uncompute (Bennett, 1973) macro to increase code reusability. Besides the above reversible hardware compatible features, it also has some reversible hardware incompatible features to meet the practical needs. For example, it views the floating-point + and − operations as reversible. It also allows users to extend instruction sets and sometimes inserting external statements. These features are not compatible with future reversible hardware. NiLang’s compiling process, grammar and operational semantics are described in Appendix G. The source code is also available online, we will put a link here after the anonymous open review session. By the time of writing, the version of NiLang is v0.7.3.

## 3.1 Reversible functions and instructions

Mathematically, any irreversible mapping `y = f(args...)` can be trivially transformed to its reversible form `y += f(args...)` or `y` ⊻ `= f(args...)` (⊻ is the bit-wise `XOR` ), where `y` is a pre-emptied variable. But in numeric computing with finite precision, this is not always true. The reversibility of arithmetic instruction is closely related to the number system. For integer and fixed

4

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

point number system, `y += f(args...)` and `y -= f(args...)` are rigorously reversible. For logarithmic number system and tropical number system (Speyer & Sturmfels, 2009), `y *= f(args...)` and `y /= f(args...)` as reversible (not introducing the zero element). While for floating point numbers, none of the above operations are rigorously reversible. However, for convenience, we ignore the round-off errors in floating-point `+` and `-` operations and treat them on equal footing with fixed-point numbers in the following discussion. In Appendix F, we will show doing this is safe in most cases provided careful implementation. Other reversible operations includes `SWAP` , `ROT` , `NEG` et. al., and this instruction set is extensible. One can define a reversible multiplier in NiLang as in Listing. 4.

Listing 4: A reversible multiplier

```
julia>usingNiLang
julia>@ifunctionmultiplier(y!::Real,a::Real,b::Real)
y!+=a*b
end
julia>multiplier(2,3,5)
(17,3,5)
julia>(~multiplier)(17,3,5)
(2,3,5)
```

Macro `@i` generates two functions that are reversible to each other, `multiplier` and ∼ `multiplier` , each defines a mapping R[3] → R[3] . The ! after a symbol is a part of the name, as a conversion to indicate the mutated variables.

## 3.2 Reversible memory management

A distinct feature of reversible memory management is that the content of a variable must be known when it is deallocated. We denote the allocation of a pre-emptied memory as `x` ← `0` , and its inverse, deallocating a **zero emptied** variable, as `x` → `0` . An unknown variable can not be deallocate, but can be pushed to a stack pop out later in the uncomputing stage. If a variable is allocated and deallocated in the local scope, we call it an ancilla. Listing. 5 defines the complex valued accumulative log function.

Here, the macro `@inline` tells the compiler that this function can be inlined. One can input “←” and “→” by typing “\leftarrow[TAB KEY]” and “\rightarrow[TAB KEY]” respectively in a Julia editor or REPL. NiLang does not have immutable structs, so that the real part `y!.re` and imaginary `y!.im` of a complex number can be changed directly. It is easy to verify that the bottom two lines in the function body are the inverse of the top two lines. i.e., the bottom two lines _uncomputes_ the top two lines. The motivation of uncomputing is to zero clear the contents in ancilla `n` so that it can be deallocated correctly. _Compute-copy-uncompute_ is a useful design pattern in reversible programming so that we created a pair of macros `@routine` and ∼ `@routine` for it. - 3.3 Reversible control flows

One can define reversible `if` , `for` and `while` statements in a reversible program. Fig. 2 (a) shows the flow chart of executing the reversible `if` statement. There are two condition expressions in this chart, a precondition and a postcondition. The precondition decides which branch to enter in the forward execution, while the postcondition decides which branch to enter in the backward execution. The pseudo-code for the forward and backward passes are shown in Listing. 7 and Listing. 8.

Fig. 2 (b) shows the flow chart of the reversible `while` statement. It also has two condition expressions. Before executing the condition expressions, the program presumes the postcondition is false. After each iteration, the program asserts the postcondition to be true. To reverse this statement, one can exchange the precondition and postcondition, and reverse the body statements. The pseudo-code for the forward and backward passes are shown in Listing. 9 and Listing. 10.

The reversible `for` statement is similar to the irreversible one except that after execution, the program will assert the iterator to be unchanged. To reverse this statement, one can exchange `start` and `stop` and inverse the sign of `step` . Listing. 11 computes the Fibonacci number recursively and reversibly.

Here, `out!` is an integer initialized to `0` for storing outputs. The precondition and postcondition are wrapped into a tuple. In the `if` statement, the postcondition is the same as the precondition, hence we omit the postcondition by inserting a "∼" in the second field for “copying the precondition in this field as the postcondition”. In the while statement, the postcondition is true only for the initial loop. Once code is proven correct, one can turn off the reversibility check by adding `@invcheckoff` before a statement. This will remove the reversibility check and make the code faster and compatible with GPU kernels (kernel functions can not handle exceptions).

## 4 Reversible automatic differentiation

- 4.1 Back propagation

We decompose the problem of reverse mode AD into two sub-problems, **reversing the code** and **computing** ∂[multiple outputs]∂[single input][.][Reversing the code is trivial in reversible programming.][Computing the] gradient here is similar to forward mode automatic differentiation that computes[∂][[][multi] ∂[single input][p][le out][p][uts][]][.] Inspired by the Julia package ForwardDiff (Revels et al., 2016), we use the operator overloading technique to differentiate the program efficiently. In the backward pass, we wrap each output variable with a composite type `GVar` that containing an extra gradient field, and feed it into the reversed generic program. Instructions are multiple dispatched to corresponding gradient instructions that update the gradient field of `GVar` at the meantime of uncomputing. By reversing this gradient program, we can obtain the gradient program for the reversed program too. One can define the adjoint (“adjoint” here means the program for back-propagating gradients) of a primitive instruction as a reversible function on **either** the function itself or its reverse since the adjoint of a function’s reverse is equivalent to the reverse of the function’s adjoint.
It can be easily verified by applying the above two mappings consecutively, which turns out to be an identity mapping considering ∂⃗[∂⃗] _[y] x_ ∂∂⃗⃗ _yx_[=][ 1][.]

The implementation details are described in Appendix C. In most languages, operator overloading is accompanied with significant overheads of function calls and object allocation and deallocation. But in a language with type inference and just in time compiling like Julia, the boundary between two approaches are vague. The compiler inlines small functions, packs an array of constant sized immutable objects into a continuous memory, and truncates unnecessary branches automatically.

## 4.2 Hessians

Combining forward mode AD and reverse mode AD is a simple yet efficient way to obtain Hessians. By wrapping the elementary type with `Dual` defined in package ForwardDiff and throwing it into the gradient program defined in NiLang, one obtains one row/column of the Hessian matrix. We will use this approach to compute Hessians in the graph embedding benchmark in Sec. D.2.

## 4.3 CUDA kernels

CUDA programming is playing a significant role in high-performance computing. In Julia, one can write GPU compatible functions in native Julia language with KernelAbstractions. (Besard et al., 2017) Since NiLang does not push variables into stack automatically for users, it is safe to write differentiable GPU kernels with NiLang. We will differentiate CUDA kernels with no more than extra 10 lines in the bundle adjustment benchmark in Sec. 5.

## 5 Benchmarks

We benchmark our framework with the state-of-the-art GP-AD frameworks, including source code transformation based Tapenade and Zygote and operator overloading based ForwardDiff and ReverseDiff. Since most tensor based AD software like famous TensorFlow and PyTorch are not designed for the using cases used in our benchmarks, we do not include those package to avoid an unfair comparison. In the following benchmarks, the CPU device is Intel(R) Xeon(R) Gold 6230 CPU @ 2.10GHz, and the GPU device is NVIDIA Titan V. For NiLang benchmarks, we have turned the reversibility check off to achieve a better performance.

We reproduced the benchmarks for Gaussian mixture model (GMM) and bundle adjustment in Srajer et al. (2018) by re-writing the programs in a reversible style. We show the results in Fig. 3. The Tapenade data is obtained by executing the docker file provided by the original benchmark, which provides a baseline for comparison.

## 6 Discussion

In this work, we demonstrate a new approach to back propagates a program called reverse computing AD by designing a reversible eDSL NiLang. NiLang is a powerful tool to differentiate code from the source code level so that can be directly useful to machine learning researches. It can generate efficient backward rules, which is exemplified in Appendix E. It can also be used to differentiate reversible neural networks like normalizing flows (Kobyzev et al., 2019) to save memory, e.g. backpropagating NICE network (Dinh et al., 2014) with only constant space overheads. NiLang is most useful in solving large scale scientific problems memory efficiently. In Liu et al. (2020), people solve the ground state problem of a 28 × 28 square lattice spin-glass by re-writing the quantum simulator with NiLang. There are some challenges in reverse computing AD too.
- The native BLAS and convolution operations in NiLang are not optimized for the memory layout, and are too slow comparing with state of the art machine learning libraries. We

9

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

- need a better implementation of these functions in the reversible programming context so that it can be more useful in training traditional deep neural networks.
- Although we show some examples of training neural networks on GPU, the shared-reading of a variable is not allowed.

- NiLang’s IR does not have variable analysis. The uncomputing pass is not always necessary for the irreversible host language to deallocate memory. In many cases, the host language’s variable analysis can figure this out, but it is not guarantted.

Another interesting issue is how to make use of reversible computing devices to save energy in machine learning. Reversible computing is not always more energy efficient than irreversible computing. In the time-space trade-off scheme in Sec. 2, we show the time to uncompute a unit of memory is exponential to _n_ as _Qn_ = (2 _k_ − 1) _[n]_ , and the computing energy also increases exponentially. On the other side, the amount of energy to erase a unit of memory is a constant. When (2 _k_ − 1) _[n]_ > 1/ξ, erasing the memory irreversibly is more energy-efficient, where ξ is the energy ratio between a reversible operation (an instruction or a gate) and its irreversible counterpart.

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper addresses a critical bottleneck in general-purpose automatic differentiation (AD): the memory and performance overhead associated with state-tracing (checkpointing) during backpropagation. The authors propose a novel solution by implementing an embedded domain-specific language (eDSL) in Julia, called NiLang, which utilizes reversible computing to traverse computational states without relying on a global stack. The conceptual shift from "storing" states to "uncomputing" them is a compelling approach to improving the efficiency of AD for non-tensor operations, particularly in scientific computing domains like sparse matrix operations and quantum simulation. The integration as an eDSL is a strategic choice, potentially allowing for better compiler optimizations and GPU kernel compatibility.

However, the paper's primary challenge lies in demonstrating the practical usability and scalability of the "reversible style." The transformation shown in Listing 1 suggests that users must write or transpile code into an augmented form ($s_{n} += f(s_{n-1})$), which essentially doubles the number of variables if not managed carefully. The authors must clarify the "time-space trade-off" mentioned in Section 2; specifically, while they claim to avoid stack overhead, the introduction of auxiliary variables to maintain reversibility could lead to its own form of memory pressure. Furthermore, the claim of "state-of-the-art performance" requires rigorous benchmarking against specialized AD tools across a broader range of applications to prove that the overhead of reversible logic (e.g., additional arithmetic to uncompute) is indeed lower than the overhead of stack-based checkpointing in production-scale simulations. Strengthening the explanation of how NiLang handles control flow (branches and loops) reversibly without significant developer burden will be essential for its adoption.

progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Progressive (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
progressive__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel approach to reverse-mode automatic differentiation (AD) by introducing a reversible embedded domain-specific language (eDSL) named NiLang, designed to eliminate the space overhead traditionally imposed by checkpointing through the use of reverse computing. The motivation is well-grounded in real limitations of existing AD systems—particularly the memory inefficiency and incompatibility with kernel-level optimizations due to implicit state caching—and the authors position their work at the intersection of AD, reversible programming, and high-performance scientific computing. The writing is mostly clear and the technical trajectory—from problem identification to language design and AD implementation—is logically structured. However, the paper suffers from several high-level issues that affect its clarity and scholarly rigor. Most notably, the transition between sections is occasionally abrupt, with key concepts like "reversible programming" and "reverse computing" introduced without sufficient formalism or intuitive explanation for readers unfamiliar with reversible computation. Furthermore, while the comparison between checkpointing and reverse computing in Section 2 is conceptually interesting, the analysis lacks mathematical precision (e.g., undefined variables, incomplete table reference) and fails to rigorously establish the claimed advantages in time-space trade-offs. The paper also overstates its novelty at times without adequately contrasting prior work in reversible AD or reversible eDSLs beyond citing their existence. Finally, although the implementation in Julia is reasonable, the absence of detailed benchmarks or performance results within the provided portion weakens the impact of the claimed state-of-the-art performance. A more careful exposition of the core reversibility mechanism, clearer evaluation criteria, and stronger theoretical justification would significantly improve the paper’s contribution and accessibility.

comments.title: Inconsistent space complexity claim for reverse computing in worst-case
comments.quote: The minimum space complexity in reverse computing is $ O(S \log( T / S )) $ (Bennett, 1989; Levine & Sherman, 1990; Perumalla, 2013), with time complexity $ O(T^{1.585}) $.
comments.explanation: The claim that reverse computing achieves $ O(S \log(T/S)) $ space and $ O(T^{1.585}) $ time appears inconsistent with the standard understanding of Bennett's algorithm. The exponent 1.585 (≈ log₂(3)) arises from a specific binary recursive implementation with a 2/1 partition (compute, copy, uncompute), which corresponds to a fixed k=2 split. However, the cited works show that the general optimal trade-off allows for $ O(S \log(T/S)) $ space and $ O(T^{1+\epsilon}) $ time for arbitrary $ \epsilon > 0 $ using k-way splitting with increasing k. The time bound $ O(T^{1.585}) $ is therefore not the minimal possible but a suboptimal case. The paper presents this as the general minimum, which misrepresents the asymptotic trade-off and could mislead readers into thinking $ T^{1.585} $ scaling is fundamental to reverse computing. This mischaracterization is reinforced by the table using $ O(T (T/S)^{0.585}) $, which equals $ O(T^{1.585}) $ when S=1. The issue lies in conflating a specific implementation with the theoretically optimal trade-off.

comments.title: Misstatement of checkpointing space overhead
comments.quote: both suffer from a space overhead that linear to time (Table 1). The checkpointing scheme snapshots the output in every step, and the reverse computing scheme allocates extra storage for storing outputs in every step.
comments.explanation: The text claims that checkpointing suffers from linear space overhead in general, which is incorrect and misleading. While the most time-efficient form of checkpointing (with no recomputation) uses $ O(T) $ space, logarithmic-space schemes like treeverse exist and are mentioned later in the paper. Table 1 also correctly shows $ O(S) $ space for space-efficient checkpointing. The prose, however, presents a blanket claim without qualification, suggesting all checkpointing methods require $ O(T) $ space. This overgeneralization could mislead readers unfamiliar with the literature. Although context partially resolves it, the sentence as written is inaccurate and should be qualified to specify it refers to the no-recomputation case.

comments.title: Incorrect binomial coefficient definition in Figure 1 caption
comments.quote:
η(τ, δ) ≡ = δ  
> (ττ+!δδ!)! is the binomial function.
comments.explanation: The definition of the binomial function η(τ, δ) is garbled due to OCR or typesetting failure. The intended expression is almost certainly the binomial coefficient $ \eta(\tau, \delta) = \binom{\tau + \delta}{\delta} $, consistent with Griewank's treeverse algorithm (1992), where the number of recursive operations grows combinatorially. The current rendering 'δ   > (ττ+!δδ!)!' is meaningless. The correct formula is $ \binom{\tau + \delta}{\delta} = \frac{(\tau + \delta)!}{\tau! \delta!} $. Although the functional role of η(t, d) is clear from context and the symbol is used consistently, the literal mathematical definition is incorrect and constitutes a significant defect in exposition for a general audience.

comments.title: Inconsistent and undefined notation in uncomputing time expression
comments.quote: we show the time to uncompute a unit of memory is exponential to _n_ as _Qn_ = (2 _k_ − 1) _[n]_
comments.explanation: The expression '_Qn_ = (2k − 1) _[n]_' is notationally inconsistent and likely erroneous. The '_[n]_' superscript is undefined and appears to be a corruption of exponentiation, possibly intended as $(2k - 1)^n$. However, this form misrepresents the actual time complexity: Bennett's recursion depends on recursion depth, not on the fixed partition count _k_. The exponential should be in the depth of recursion (e.g., $ O(2^d) $), not in _k_, which is constant per level. This misstatement undermines the accuracy of the energy-efficiency analysis in Section 71. Even under charitable interpretation, the formula is both ambiguous and mathematically incorrect as presented.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of energy efficiency in reversible computing
comments.quote: On the other hand, the amount of energy to erase a unit of memory is a constant. When (2 _k_ − 1) _[n]_ > 1/ξ, erasing the memory irreversibly is more energy-efficient, where ξ is the energy ratio between a reversible operation (an instruction or a gate) and its irreversible counterpart.
comments.explanation: The paper attempts to compare energy costs using Landauer's principle, stating irreversible erasure has constant cost while reversible uncomputing scales with time complexity $(2k - 1)^n$. However, equating algorithmic time complexity directly with energy expenditure oversimplifies thermodynamics. Reversible operations are typically modeled below the Landauer limit, but the ratio ξ is not a standard or well-defined parameter that allows such a simple threshold condition. The claim suggests time efficiency directly determines energy dominance, but energy and time are distinct resources. While the broader insight—that reversibility isn't universally energy-optimal—is valid, the quantitative justification is flawed and lacks grounding in reversible computing models. This weakens an otherwise valuable discussion on trade-offs.

comments.title: Ambiguous Gradient Computation Description

comments.explanation: The gradient computation description contains a severely mangled OCR artifact: the expression `∂[multiple outputs]/∂[single input]` is broken and rendered as `[∂][[][multi] ∂[single input][p][le out][p][uts][]]`, making the mathematical intent illegible. While this is a plausible and known technique, the garbled notation prevents verification. The claim that the method is 'similar to forward mode' further risks conceptual confusion unless clarified. A reader cannot confirm the method or reproduce the approach from this rendering. The issue is primarily expository but undermines technical credibility.

comments.title: Underspecified GVar Gradient Propagation Mechanism
comments.quote: Instructions are multiple dispatched to corresponding gradient instructions that update the gradient field of `GVar` at the meantime of uncomputing.
comments.explanation: The mechanism for gradient propagation during uncomputation is insufficiently described. The paper states that gradient fields of `GVar` are updated via multiple dispatch but omits critical details: how gradient accumulation is orchestrated reversibly, whether updates are tied to forward or reverse instructions, how adjoint values are stored without an implicit stack, and how the chain rule is applied. Standard reverse-mode AD uses tapes or tapes-like structures, but NiLang avoids these. The implication is that the reverse execution drives gradient updates, but this risks double-counting if not carefully designed. The paper does not clarify whether this is a source-to-source transformation or runtime dual-number style. Although Appendix C may contain details, the main text lacks even a sketch of the core innovation, hindering understanding and reproducibility of their AD approach.

comments.title: Incorrect Identity Claim for Adjoint and Reverse Composition
comments.quote: It can be easily verified by applying the above two mappings consecutively, which turns out to be an identity mapping considering ∂⃗[∂⃗] _[y] x_ ∂∂⃗⃗ _yx_[=][ 1][.]
comments.explanation: The paper claims that composing the adjoint and reverse mappings results in an identity, justified by a malformed expression suggestive of ∂²y/∂x∂y = 1, which is mathematically invalid and dimensionally incoherent. The core assertion—that (f⁻¹)† ≡ (f†)⁻¹—is not generally true for arbitrary differentiable functions. The adjoint applies transposed Jacobians, while reverse applies functional inversion under reversibility constraints. These operations do not commute without strong conditions (e.g., linearity, unitarity). The claim may hold within NiLang's restricted reversible subset due to design invariants, but this requires justification, not bald assertion. The use of a flawed second-derivative identity as proof further weakens the argument. This overstates the generality of their AD model and risks misleading readers about compositional correctness.

comments.quote: It can be easily verified by applying the above two mappings consecutively, which turns out to be an identity mapping considering ∂⃗[∂⃗] _[y] x_ ∂∂⃗
comments.explanation: The concluding expression '∂⃗[∂⃗] _[y] x_ ∂∂⃗' is a severely mangled OCR artifact or incomplete LaTeX that fails to express any verifiable mathematical identity. It likely intends to support the earlier claim about adjoint-reverse composition being an identity, possibly referencing $\text{adjoint}(f^{-1}) = (\text{adjoint}(f))^{-1}$. However, the current form is unreadable and cannot be interpreted under any standard notation. This undermines the paper's emphasis on formal correctness, as readers cannot verify the claimed 'easy' proof. While the intent may be recoverable from context, the expression itself is a technical defect in exposition.

comments.title: Inconsistent terminology: 'zero emptied' vs. 'zero-cleared' or 'cleared to zero'
comments.quote: its inverse, deallocating a **zero emptied** variable, as `x` → `0`
comments.explanation: The term 'zero emptied' is non-standard and misleading in the context of reversible computing. Standard terminology includes 'zero-cleared', 'cleaned', or 'initialized to zero'. 'Emptied' inaccurately suggests passive discard of content, whereas reversible deallocation requires active erasure (uncomputation) to a known zero state. Later in the same section, the phrase 'zero clear the contents' is used, creating internal inconsistency. While the notation (`x → 0`) is correct and the core concept is sound, the terminology is awkward and potentially confusing. This is a minor stylistic and pedagogical issue but detracts from precision in a field where language carries technical weight.

comments.title: Misleading Performance Claim in GMM Benchmark
comments.quote: NiLang’s objective function is ∼2× slower than normal code due to the uncomputing overhead. In this case, NiLang does not show advantage to Tapenade in obtaining gradients, the ratio between computing the gradients and the objective function are close.
comments.explanation: While the paper accurately reports that NiLang's GMM objective function is ~2x slower and offers no gradient computation advantage over Tapenade, it contrasts with the broader 'state-of-the-art performance' claim in the abstract. In GMM, where matrix-vector multiplication dominates and Tapenade uses optimized BLAS-level rules, NiLang's scalar-level reversible overhead provides no benefit. The performance claim is thus overstated for CPU-based, linear-algebra-heavy models. The paper acknowledges the bottleneck but does not qualify the 'state-of-the-art' assertion to reflect that NiLang excels primarily in GPU kernels or memory-bound, highly parallel tasks (like bundle adjustment). This is a framing issue: the work is strong in niche domains, but the claim lacks appropriate scope limitation.

This could mislead readers into thinking that reverse computing fundamentally has $ T^{1.585} $ scaling, when better trade-offs exist. The context does not resolve this—it presents it as a general lower bound, which it is not.

comments.title: Misstatement of checkpointing space overhead
comments.quote: both suffer from a space overhead that linear to time (Table 1). The checkpointing scheme snapshots the output in every step, and the reverse computing scheme allocates extra storage for storing outputs in every step.
comments.explanation: The text claims that both methods suffer from linear space overhead in the general case, which contradicts the well-known existence of logarithmic-space checkpointing schemes like treeverse. While the statement might be intended to describe the 'most time-efficient' setting (where no recomputation is used), it immediately follows with a discussion of the 'most space-efficient' case, creating ambiguity. However, the authors later correctly describe the treeverse algorithm that achieves logarithmic space, and Table 1 shows $ O(S) $ space for the space-efficient checkpointing case. So the issue lies in the prose: saying 'both suffer from a space overhead linear to time' is inaccurate for checkpointing in general. This could mislead a reader into thinking that any form of checkpointing requires $ O(T) $ space. The correct interpretation is that the most time-efficient form (no recomputation) uses $ O(T) $ space, but the statement lacks that qualifier. While specialists might infer the intended meaning, the sentence as written is misleading and overgeneralized. The context partially resolves it (via Table 1 and the treeverse explanation), but the standalone claim is still problematic.

comments.title: Redundant word and possible OCR duplication in algorithm description
comments.quote: It evenly **evenly** partition the program into _k_ sectors.
comments.explanation: The word 'evenly' appears twice, which is almost certainly a typo (possibly OCR duplication or editing error). The intended phrase is clearly 'It evenly partitions the program into k sectors.' This does not affect technical meaning, as the repetition is in natural language and the meaning remains clear. The rest of the description—Bennett's algorithm using uniform partitioning—is correct. Given that OCR errors are expected and the meaning is unambiguous, this is not a substantive issue. The authors correctly associate the algorithm with compute-copy-uncompute and describe the recursive structure properly in Listing 3. This is purely a linguistic artifact and does not impede understanding or reproducibility.

comments.title: Incorrect binomial coefficient definition in Figure 1 caption
comments.quote:
η(τ, δ) ≡ = δ  
> (ττ+!δδ!)! is the binomial function.
comments.explanation: The definition of the binomial function η(τ, δ) is garbled due to OCR or typesetting failure. The intended expression is almost certainly the binomial coefficient $ \eta(\tau, \delta) = \binom{\tau + \delta}{\delta} $, as this matches Griewank's treeverse algorithm (1992), where the number of operations grows combinatorially. The current text shows 'δ   > (ττ+!δδ!)!', which appears to be a corrupted version of a LaTeX binomial (e.g., \binom{\tau + \delta}{\delta}) or an expression involving factorials. The correct formula is $ \binom{\tau + \delta}{\delta} = \frac{(\tau + \delta)!}{\tau! \delta!} $. While the functional role of η(t, d) is clear from context (as a scaling function for recursive partitioning), the literal definition as printed is meaningless. This is more than a formatting issue—it misrepresents a key mathematical object. However, since the symbol η(t,d) is used consistently in the recursive application of treeverse, and the algorithmic description is otherwise accurate, a reader familiar with the literature could reconstruct the intended meaning. Still, for a general audience, this is a significant technical defect in exposition.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of Bennett's algorithm in Listing 3
comments.quote: bennett(` { _s_ 1, ...} `, k, i, len) if len == 1` _si_ +1 ← `0` _fi_ `(` _si_ +1 `,` _si_ `) else # P process that calls the forward program k steps bennett(` { _s_ 1, ...} `, k, i+len` ÷ `k*(j-1), len` ÷ `k) for j=1,2,...,k # Q process that calls the backward program k-1 steps ~bennett(` { _s_ 1, ...} `, k, i+len` ÷ `k*(j-1), len` ÷ `k) for j=k-1,k-2,...,1`
comments.explanation: The pseudocode in Listing 3 is underspecified. The recursive calls in the 'P process' loop use `i+len ÷ k*(j-1)`, which is ambiguous: is it `i + (len ÷ k)*(j-1)` or `i + len ÷ (k*(j-1))`? The former is intended, as it computes the starting index of the j-th sector. But without parentheses, and given the linear progression of j, it's syntactically unclear. Similarly, the base case: `_fi_ (` likely is an OCR error for `f_i(`, meaning the forward function at step i. But `fi` is not defined. The loop structure also lacks explicit block delimiters (e.g., 'end'), making scoping ambiguous. The reversal uses `~bennett` in a loop, which implies inverting each recursive call, but without clear control flow, it's hard to verify correctness. The algorithm as written cannot be directly implemented without interpretation. While the accompanying text and figure help, the pseudocode itself is underspecified. A reader would struggle to reproduce the exact recursion. This is a real issue for reproducibility, though likely due to space constraints or OCR. The core idea—recursive compute and uncompute—is clear, but key boundary conditions and arithmetic are ambiguous.

comments.title: Ambiguous claim about rigorous reversibility of floating-point + and - despite ignoring round-off errors
comments.quote: While for floating point numbers, none of the above operations are rigorously reversible. However, for convenience, we ignore the round-off errors in floating-point `+` and `-` operations and treat them on equal footing with fixed-point numbers in the following discussion.
comments.explanation:
At first glance, this statement appears internally consistent: the authors correctly acknowledge that floating-point addition and subtraction are not mathematically reversible due to rounding errors and finite precision, which is a well-known fact in numerical analysis and reversible computing. They then state they will treat them as reversible 'for convenience,' which aligns with their stated assumption (Assumption 5 in full context): 'Floating-point + and − are treated as reversible in NiLang for practicality, though not strictly reversible on future reversible hardware.' This assumption is justified pragmatically — for existing hardware, exact reversibility is less critical than performance, and small numerical discrepancies may be tolerable in gradient computation.

However, the concern arises from the phrase 'treat them on equal footing with fixed-point numbers.' Fixed-point arithmetic, like integer arithmetic, supports truly rigorous reversibility under the +/− model because it avoids the non-associativity and precision loss inherent in floating-point. By equating their behavior, the statement risks misleading readers into thinking that floating-point reversibility is as sound as fixed-point, which it is not — the error is qualitative, not just quantitative. While Appendix F is cited to justify safety in practice, the main text does not clarify that this equivalence is only approximate and context-dependent. A careful reader might reasonably conclude that floating-point +/− are as reversible as integer operations in NiLang, which overstates the robustness of the implementation.

This is not a mathematical error per se, but a **framing issue** that could mislead about correctness guarantees. The context resolves part of the concern, but the strong equivalence claim remains questionable without qualification.

comments.title: Inconsistent terminology: 'zero emptied' vs. 'zero-cleared' or 'cleared to zero'
comments.quote: its inverse, deallocating a **zero emptied** variable, as `x` → `0`
comments.explanation:
The term 'zero emptied' is used to describe a variable that has been cleared to zero before deallocation, which is a core concept in reversible memory management — one must only deallocate memory in a known (typically zero) state. The intended meaning is clear from context: `x → 0` is the inverse of `x ← 0`, and safety depends on ensuring `x` contains zero at deallocation.

However, 'zero emptied' is non-standard and awkward terminology. The standard terms in reversible computing are 'zero-cleared' (Bennett, 1989), 'cleaned', or 'initialized to zero'. 'Emptied' suggests removal of content, but in reversible computing, the content must be **explicitly erased** (uncomputed) to zero, not just discarded. The phrasing could mislead a reader into thinking the variable is simply freed regardless of content, which contradicts the central principle.

Later in the same section, the phrase 'zero clear the contents' is used ('zero clear the contents in ancilla `n`'), which is more accurate. This inconsistency between 'zero emptied' and 'zero clear' is minor but noticeable. Given that the notation (`x ← 0` and `x → 0`) is precisely defined and used correctly in all subsequent examples, the **core concept is sound**. This is purely a **stylistic and terminological inconsistency**, not a technical flaw.

comments.title: Misleading presentation of 'reversible' multiplier in Listing 4
comments.quote:
julia>@ifunctionmultiplier(y!::Real,a::Real,b::Real)
y!+=a*b
end
julia>multiplier(2,3,5)
(17,3,5)
julia>(~multiplier)(17,3,5)
(2,3,5)
comments.explanation:
At first glance, the example appears to demonstrate a reversible multiplier: applying the forward and reverse functions returns to the original state. This seems correct and aligns with the language design.

However, a deeper inspection reveals a fundamental issue: the operation `y! += a*b` is **not logically reversible** unless the original value of `y!` is preserved or recoverable. In this function, the input `y!` is mutated in place, and there is no mechanism shown to recover its initial value after the forward pass — yet the reverse operation purports to fully invert it.

The example output shows `(2,3,5)` returned from `(~multiplier)(17,3,5)`. This implies that the reverse function subtracts `a*b = 15` from `y! = 17` to get `2`, which works only if the original `y!` value (`2`) is not otherwise lost. But in true reversible computing, the transformation must preserve all information necessary for inversion. Here, the reversal depends on the **final state** and the **inputs** `a` and `b`, which are unchanged. Since `a` and `b` are read-only, and the operation is `y! += a*b`, the reverse is indeed `y! -= a*b`, which is possible because `a` and `b` remain available.

So **this is actually valid** under NiLang's model: inputs are preserved, and only the accumulator is mutated. The reverse operation uses the same `a` and `b` to uncompute the product. The function is reversible as long as `a` and `b` are not modified.

The potential confusion arises because a reader might interpret `y!` as being overwritten irreversibly, but in fact, the operation is designed like an affine reversible gate (e.g., `ADD` in a register machine). The example **works as intended** and matches standard practices in reversible eDSLs (e.g., Bennett's model).

After careful analysis, no actual error is found. The example is correct, and the concern is resolved by context. However, a brief clarification (e.g., 'reversibility is ensured because input arguments remain unmodified') would improve clarity. As is, this is not a flaw, but the presentation skirts the edge of ambiguity.

comments.title: Inconsistent use of notation in Listing 5 and surrounding text
comments.quote:
n ←`zero(T)`
n += abs(x)
y!.re += log(n)
y!.im += angle(x)
n -= abs(x)
n →`zero(T)`
comments.explanation:
The variable `n` is first allocated with `n ← zero(T)`, then updated with `n += abs(x)`. The reverse operations are `n -= abs(x)` and `n → zero(T)`. This appears internally consistent: allocate, compute, uncompute, deallocate.

However, the initial allocation uses `zero(T)`, which is presumably a zero value of type `T`. Then `abs(x)` is added to `n`. The issue is that `n -= abs(x)` does not necessarily return `n` to exactly zero unless floating-point operations were perfectly reversible — which the authors have already admitted they are not. So while the structure is correct in the abstract (allocate → use → uncompute → deallocate), the **numerical feasibility** of `n -= abs(x)` restoring `n` to zero depends on the floating-point reversibility assumption.

But this is already acknowledged two paragraphs earlier: 'we ignore the round-off errors in floating-point + and - operations...' So the example assumes that `n += abs(x)` can be undone by `n -= abs(x)`, which is consistent with their modeling choice. The **notation and flow are correct**, and the example matches the design.

An OCR artifact may be present in the original PDF: in the quote, `zero(T)` appears as such, but in the surrounding text, it's rendered with backticks. No symbol inconsistency is evident. The concern is resolved by context — the numerical reversal is assumed to work by fiat, with justification deferred to Appendix F. This is acceptable given the paper’s stated assumptions.

comments.title: Confusing labeling in Listing 5 and Listing 6 with split-table formatting
comments.quote: Listing 5: Reversible complex valued log Listing 6: Compute-copy-uncompute verfunction _y_ += log(| _x_ |) + _i_ Arg( _x_ ). sion of Listing. 5
comments.explanation:
The formatting of Listing 5 and Listing 6 is problematic due to a split-table layout in the OCR-extracted text. The title appears to conflate both listings: 'Reversible complex valued log' and then immediately 'Compute-copy-uncompute verfunction... sion of Listing. 5', creating a **typographical run-on title**.

Initially, this suggests that Listing 5 is defining a function called `verfunction`, which is not correct. But inspection of the code blocks shows that:
- Left column: Listing 5 — direct implementation with explicit allocate, compute, uncompute, deallocate.
- Right column: Listing 6 — version using `@routine` and `~@routine` macros.

The real issue is **formatting loss from OCR**: the intended layout was likely two side-by-side code blocks with separate headings, but the text merge has created a misleading title. The actual function is `(:+=)(log)`, not `verfunction`. The phrase 'verfunction _y_ += ...' seems to be a corrupted version of 'function _y_ += ...' with 'ver' prepended (possibly a typo for 'reversible'). This is almost certainly an **OCR artifact**, not an author error.

Given the note in the instructions to consider OCR misreads, and the high likelihood that 'verfunction' was meant to be 'function', this is **not a substantive issue**. The code is correct and clear in both listings. The confusion is due to extraction artifacts, not the authors’ content.

comments.title: Ambiguous Gradient Computation Description
comments.quote: Computing the gradient here is similar to forward mode automatic differentiation that computes[∂][[][multi] ∂[single input][p][le out][p][uts][]][.]
comments.explanation: The OCR artifact in the passage — particularly the mangled LaTeX-like expression `∂[multiple outputs]∂[single input]` and the broken rendering of `[∂][[][multi] ∂[single input][p][le out][p][uts][]]` — initially suggests a serious notation error or conceptual confusion in how gradients are computed. At first glance, it appears the authors may be conflating Jacobian-vector products (reverse mode) with vector-Jacobian products (forward mode), or misrepresenting the dimensionality of the gradient. However, examining the surrounding context reveals that the intended meaning is likely: computing gradients in the backward pass via reverse mode AD, but *using techniques analogous to forward mode*, specifically by overloading operations to propagate gradient updates during uncomputation. The mention of `GVar` with a gradient field and comparison to `ForwardDiff` supports this: they are using forward-mode-like operator overloading within a reverse-mode framework — a known technique in some AD systems (e.g., 'dual number reversal'). The core issue is that the mangled math notation, likely due to PDF/OCR failure, obscures an otherwise plausible method. While the *intent* aligns with standard practices, the current rendering is so garbled that a reader cannot confirm the mathematical claim. A clean version should clarify whether they compute ∂(multiple outputs)/∂(single input) (i.e., a gradient vector), and explicitly state that the forward-mode-like mechanism is applied during the reverse pass for efficiency, which is a subtle but important point.

comments.title: Incorrect Identity Claim for Adjoint and Reverse Composition
comments.quote: It can be easily verified by applying the above two mappings consecutively, which turns out to be an identity mapping considering ∂⃗[∂⃗] _[y] x_ ∂∂⃗⃗ _yx_[=][ 1][.]
comments.explanation: The claim that composing 'adjoint' and 'reverse' mappings results in an identity, justified by ∂²y/∂x² = 1, is mathematically unsound. First, the expression `∂⃗[∂⃗] _[y] x_ ∂∂⃗⃗ _yx_ [=][ 1]` is malformed, but appears to suggest ∂²y/∂x∂y = 1 or similar, which is not generally true and dimensionally incoherent. The key issue is conceptual: the authors assert that the adjoint of a reverse function equals the reverse of the adjoint function — symbolically, (f⁻¹)† ≡ (f†)⁻¹ — and that composing these operations yields an identity. While this may hold for specific linear, unitary, or involutive operations, it is *not* universally true for arbitrary differentiable functions in AD. For instance, if f is non-invertible or has non-symmetric Jacobians, the reverse (f⁻¹) may not exist or may differ significantly from the adjoint (f†). The adjoint represents transposed Jacobian application, while the reverse represents functional inversion under reversibility constraints. Equating their compositions is a strong claim requiring rigorous proof, especially in a general-purpose AD context. The paper provides only a hand-waving reference to a second derivative identity, which lacks relevance (mixed partials of a variable over itself don't generally equal 1). The claim might hold within the constrained, reversible subset of NiLang programs due to design choices (e.g., structured inverses), but this requires justification, not assertion. Without it, this overstates the generality of their AD design.

comments.title: Misleading Performance Claim in GMM Benchmark
comments.quote: NiLang’s objective function is ∼2× slower than normal code due to the uncomputing overhead. In this case, NiLang does not show advantage to Tapenade in obtaining gradients, the ratio between computing the gradients and the objective function are close.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly observe that NiLang's objective function is about 2x slower and that the gradient computation overhead is similar to Tapenade's, implying no speed advantage. However, the broader claim in the abstract — that the framework achieves 'state-of-the-art performance' — is questionable given this result. In the GMM benchmark, which reuses expensive linear algebra (matrix-vector multiplication), traditional AD tools like Tapenade can differentiate efficiently using pre-defined high-performance rules. NiLang, operating at the scalar level with reversible uncomputation overhead, cannot compete. While NiLang excels in GPU kernels (as shown in bundle adjustment), the claim of 'state-of-the-art' is overstated when, on CPU and for common ML models like GMM, it is merely competitive or slower. The paper acknowledges matrix-vector multiplication as the bottleneck, suggesting NiLang provides no advantage where BLAS dominates. A careful reader might conclude the 'state-of-the-art' claim is supported only in niche, memory-bound, or GPU-parallelizable cases, not broadly. This is a framing issue: the work is strong, but the claim overreaches given the presented benchmarks.

comments.title: Underspecified GVar Gradient Propagation Mechanism
comments.quote: Instructions are multiple dispatched to corresponding gradient instructions that update the gradient field of `GVar` at the meantime of uncomputing.
comments.explanation: The description of how gradients are computed during uncomputation is too vague for replication. The paper states that `GVar` wraps output variables and that gradient fields are updated via multiple dispatch during uncomputation, but it omits the core mechanism: *how* is the gradient information accumulated reversibly? In standard reverse mode AD, gradients are accumulated via a tape or stack, but NiLang avoids such implicit storage. The authors imply that by running the reversed program with `GVar`, the side effects during uncompute automatically propagate gradients — but this requires a specific design: likely, the primal operation (e.g., `y! += a*b`) has an associated gradient rule (e.g., updating `∂L/∂a += ∂L/∂y * b`), and during reverse execution (`y! -= a*b`), the same rule is applied. However, this would double-count or misalign updates unless carefully orchestrated. The paper does not specify whether gradient updates are attached to forward or reverse instructions, how adjoint values are stored and accessed reversibly, or how the chain rule is applied without an implicit stack. While Appendix C is referenced, the main text should at minimum sketch whether this is a source-to-source transformation or a runtime dual-number style approach. This is critical for understanding the novelty and correctness of their AD implementation.

comments.title: Ambiguous Gradient Transformation Formula
comments.quote: It can be easily verified by applying the above two mappings consecutively, which turns out to be an identity mapping considering ∂⃗[∂⃗] _[y] x_ ∂∂⃗
comments.explanation: The concluding expression '∂⃗[∂⃗] _[y] x_ ∂∂⃗' is highly ambiguous and appears to be a severely mangled OCR artifact or an incomplete LaTeX expression. It likely intends to express some form of gradient consistency or adjoint identity, such as the equivalence between the adjoint of a reverse and the reverse of an adjoint, referenced earlier in the paper summary as $\text{adjoint}(f^{-1}) = (\text{adjoint}(f))^{-1}$. The surrounding text claims that this identity ensures compositional correctness in gradient propagation, which is a valid and important point in reversible AD. However, this garbled formula fails to substantiate that claim and instead introduces confusion. Given the paper's emphasis on formal correctness in reversible transformations, the inability to clearly express this key mathematical verification undermines the technical rigor. While the intended meaning can be inferred from the context and the earlier theorem statement, the malformed expression itself is a real problem — it does not resolve upon deeper inspection and cannot be interpreted as a notational variation even under generous assumptions. A reader cannot verify the 'easy' verification the authors refer to.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Energy Efficiency in Reversible Computing
comments.quote: On the other hand, the amount of energy to erase a unit of memory is a constant. When (2 _k_ − 1) _[n]_ > 1/ξ, erasing the memory irreversibly is more energy-efficient, where ξ is the energy ratio between a reversible operation (an instruction or a gate) and its irreversible counterpart.
comments.explanation: The passage attempts to compare energy costs between reversible uncomputing and irreversible erasure. It states that irreversible erasure has constant energy cost (consistent with Landauer's principle), while reversible uncomputing in Bennett's scheme has time scaling as $Q_n = (2k - 1)^n$, which it treats as proportional to energy. While the asymptotic time cost of Bennett's recursive scheme can indeed be exponential in depth, equating this directly to energy via $ (2k−1)^n > 1/\xi $ oversimplifies the physical reality. In reversible computing models, the energy of a reversible operation is typically modeled as being below the Landauer limit, but not necessarily constant or directly comparable via a fixed ratio $\xi$ to irreversible operations. Moreover, the condition presented suggests that irreversible erasure becomes preferable when uncomputing time is large — but this trades time for energy without considering the actual thermodynamic cost per operation. The analysis neglects that Landauer’s limit is about entropy reduction, not time, and conflates algorithmic time complexity with energy expenditure. While the broader point — that reversibility isn't always energy-optimal under all trade-offs — is valid and insightful, the quantitative justification is flawed. The claim overreaches by implying a simple threshold condition based on time complexity and an abstract $\xi$, which lacks grounding in standard models of reversible energy consumption.

comments.title: Inconsistent and Undefined Notation in Uncomputing Time Expression
comments.quote: we show the time to uncompute a unit of memory is exponential to _n_ as _Qn_ = (2 _k_ − 1) _[n]_
comments.explanation: The expression '_Qn_ = (2 _k_ − 1) _[n]_' is notationally inconsistent and undefined. The '_[n]_' superscript is not explained and does not match any prior or subsequent notation in the paper. It appears to intend exponentiation, i.e., $(2k - 1)^n$, which would align with the known time complexity of Bennett’s recursion. However, as written, '_[n]_' could be misread as an index, a footnoted expression, or an OCR corruption (e.g., of '^n'). The variable _k_ is also not locally defined in this context; while _k_ was previously used in Sec. 2 as the number of partitions in Bennett’s algorithm, that usage makes $(2k - 1)^n$ dimensionally incorrect — _k_ is fixed per partitioning, not growing with recursion depth _n_. The exponential should be in the depth of recursion, not in a fixed _k_. Thus, the formula is both notationally unclear and likely mathematically incorrect as presented. Even under best reading (assuming '_[n]_' means 'to the power n'), the dependence on _k_ rather than recursion depth misrepresents the actual time complexity, which is typically $O(2^d)$ for depth _d_. This misstatement weakens an otherwise valuable discussion on energy trade-offs in reversible computing.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a novel approach to general-purpose automatic differentiation by utilizing a reversible programming eDSL (NiLang) to mitigate the memory overhead and stack manipulation costs associated with traditional reverse-mode AD. While the core idea of leveraging reverse computing for bi-directional execution without implicit stacks is promising, the manuscript is marred by substandard writing quality, featuring numerous grammatical errors (e.g., "Embeded," "general purposed") and awkward phrasing that detract from its professional credibility. A critical clarification is required regarding the space complexity; the abstract implies a solution to space overhead, yet Section 2 concedes that reverse computing still suffers from space overhead linear to time, creating a contradiction that undermines the paper's value proposition. Additionally, the authors must better address the practical usability of their framework, as the requirement to write or transpile code into a reversible style imposes a significant burden on the user that may outweigh the performance benefits, making the claim of "differentiating everything" appear hyperbolic without further evidence of automation or ease of integration.

comments.title: Notation errors and inconsistency in complexity analysis
comments.explanation: There are notation errors in Table 1 (e.g., 'T_S' likely meaning T/S, and formatting issues like 'O(T[1][.][585])'). This results in an inconsistency where the table lists the time complexity as O(T(T/S)^0.585) while the text states O(T^1.585). These are only equivalent if S=1. The notation needs correction and the discrepancy between the text and table must be resolved.

comments.title: Undefined term 'binomially' in treeverse description
comments.quote: This algorithm partitions the computational process binomially into d sectors
comments.explanation: The term 'binomially' is used to describe how the treeverse algorithm partitions the computation, but this is not defined or explained. The subsequent formula appears to be garbled due to formatting issues, making it impossible to verify the claim about binomial partitioning. This leaves the reader without a clear understanding of how the treeverse algorithm actually works.

comments.title: Ambiguous operator precedence in Bennett's algorithm
comments.quote: bennett({ s_1, ...} , k, i+len ÷ k*(j-1), len ÷ k)
comments.explanation: The pseudo-code uses a non-standard division symbol ('÷') and lacks parentheses, creating ambiguous operator precedence in the expression `i+len ÷ k*(j-1)`. It is unclear whether this means `(i+len) ÷ (k*(j-1))` or `i + (len ÷ k)*(j-1)`. This ambiguity must be resolved for correct implementation of the algorithm.

comments.title: Questionable claim about zero time overhead
comments.quote: It is easy to see, both checkpointing and reverse computing can trace back states without time overhead, but both suffer from a space overhead that linear to time (Table 1).
comments.explanation: The claim that both methods can trace back states 'without time overhead' contradicts the immediately following discussion and Table 1, which shows time complexities greater than O(T) for space-efficient variants. Even the 'most time efficient' column shows O(T) time complexity, which is the same as the original computation, not zero overhead. This appears to be an oversimplification that could mislead readers about the actual costs involved.

comments.title: Repeated word in description
comments.quote: It evenly evenly partition the program into k sectors.
comments.explanation: The word 'evenly' is repeated. This appears to be a simple typographical error where 'evenly partition' was intended.

comments.title: Unusual term 'easing through uncomputing'
comments.quote: One may argue that easing through uncomputing is not necessary here.
comments.explanation: The term 'easing through uncomputing' appears to be unusual in the context of reversible computing. Based on the surrounding text discussing erasing computed results, this likely should be 'erasing through uncomputing'. While the meaning can be inferred from context, the non-standard terminology could confuse readers.

comments.title: Floating-point reversibility claim needs stronger justification
comments.quote: However, for convenience, we ignore the round-off errors in floating-point `+` and `-` operations and treat them on equal footing with fixed-point numbers in the following discussion. In Appendix F, we will show doing this is safe in most cases provided careful implementation.
comments.explanation: The authors acknowledge that floating-point operations aren't rigorously reversible due to round-off errors, but then claim this approach is 'safe in most cases.' This is a significant claim that affects the correctness of their AD implementation. While they reference Appendix F for justification, the statement 'safe in most cases' is quite strong and potentially misleading. Floating-point round-off errors can accumulate in numerical computations, and for scientific computing applications where precision matters, this could lead to incorrect gradients. The authors should either provide stronger justification here or qualify their claim more carefully.

comments.title: Potentially incorrect Fibonacci implementation
comments.quote:
Listing 11: Computing Fibonacci number recursively and reversibly.

`@i function rrfib(out!, n) @invcheckoff if (n >= 1, ~) counter` ← `0 counter += n while (counter > 1, counter!=n) rrfib(out!, counter-1) counter -= 2 end counter -= n % 2 counter` → `0 end out! += 1 end`
comments.explanation: The Fibonacci implementation appears unusual and potentially incorrect. The function initializes a counter to 0, adds n to it, then while counter > 1 and counter != n, recursively computes Fibonacci with counter-1. The logic for computing Fibonacci numbers isn't clear from this implementation. A standard Fibonacci implementation would typically involve recursive calls with n-1 and n-2. This example might confuse readers about how reversible programming works, and if incorrect, could undermine confidence in the examples provided.

comments.title: Insufficient detail on gradient computation mechanism
comments.quote: Computing the gradient here is similar to forward mode automatic differentiation... Inspired by the Julia package ForwardDiff (Revels et al., 2016), we use the operator overloading technique to differentiate the program efficiently.
comments.explanation: The description of how gradients are computed is vague. While they mention using operator overloading inspired by ForwardDiff, they don't explain how gradients are accumulated, how the adjoint of primitive instructions is defined, or specifically how updates occur 'at the meantime of uncomputing.' This section feels underspecified for readers who want to understand how the AD actually works in practice.

comments.title: Ambiguous statement about tropical number system reversibility
comments.quote: For logarithmic number system and tropical number system (Speyer & Sturmfels, 2009), `y *= f(args...)` and `y /= f(args...)` as reversible (not introducing the zero element).
comments.explanation: The statement about tropical number system reversibility contains a grammatical error ('as reversible' instead of 'are reversible') and is potentially misleading. The tropical number system has unique properties where addition and multiplication are redefined, and claiming that multiplication and division are reversible without introducing the zero element needs more explanation. The parenthetical note about 'not introducing the zero element' is unclear without additional context about how zero is handled in tropical arithmetic.

comments.title: Corrupted mathematical notation in AD section

progressive_original__glm-4.6.label: Progressive Original (glm-4.6)
progressive_original__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a novel approach to general-purpose automatic differentiation by utilizing a reversible programming eDSL (NiLang) to mitigate the memory overhead and stack manipulation costs associated with traditional reverse-mode AD. A critical clarification is required regarding the space complexity; the abstract implies a solution to space overhead, yet Section 2 concedes that reverse computing still suffers from space overhead linear to time, creating a contradiction that undermines the paper's value proposition. Additionally, the authors must better address the practical usability of their framework, as the requirement to write or transpile code into a reversible style imposes a significant burden on the user that may outweigh the performance benefits, making the claim of "differentiating everything" appear hyperbolic without further evidence of automation or ease of integration.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper presents an intriguing, high-level architectural approach to automatic differentiation (AD) by leveraging reversibility to bypass the memory overheads inherent in traditional checkpointing. The core motivation—addressing the scalability of general-purpose AD for complex scientific computing—is well-justified and positions the work as a potentially significant contribution beyond standard machine learning applications. However, the manuscript requires significant refinement to meet the rigorous standards of top-tier conferences like ICLR. Most crucially, the technical depth is currently insufficient; the transition from the theoretical advantages of reversible programming to the specific design of the "NiLang" eDSL remains vague. The text would benefit from a concrete illustrative example of the eDSL's syntax and how it handles common programming patterns (like branching or control flow) that are notoriously difficult to reverse. Furthermore, the writing suffers from minor grammatical inconsistencies and a lack of precise formalization in the provided "Listing" examples. To strengthen the paper, the authors should prioritize a more rigorous comparison with existing AD frameworks, moving beyond descriptive claims toward a nuanced performance analysis that clarifies exactly where these reversible transformations yield practical speedups versus where they introduce prohibitive computational overhead.

comments.title: Inconsistent or corrupted mathematical notation

comments.explanation: Several core formal definitions are unintelligible, likely due to OCR errors or poorly formatted LaTeX. This includes the definition of the binomial function $\eta(\tau, \delta)$, the identity mapping proof for adjoints, and the energy scaling relation $Q_n$. These must be corrected to maintain the paper's theoretical credibility.

comments.title: Ambiguity and lack of formal rigor in Bennett's algorithm implementation

Furthermore, the logic for reversing while-loops in Listing 10 incorrectly asserts preconditions based on inverted postconditions, which lacks the formal rigor necessary for a reversible eDSL definition.

comments.explanation: The paper makes strong claims about floating-point reversibility, constant memory usage, and memory safety during gradient transformation without providing the necessary theoretical or empirical justification. Specifically, treating lossy floating-point operations as reversible and claiming 'register-only' allocation for complex benchmarks requires more rigorous proof to be credible.
